agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Immunoglobulin M -- Clinical Significance -- Selective IgM Deficiency
agent1_snippets.content: The binding of IgM to erythrocytes activates the classical complement pathway, resulting in the attachment of the opsonin C3b to the erythrocyte. This IgM-mediated C3b binding to the erythrocyte is stable even when the erythrocyte returns closer to core body temperatures, resulting in dissociation of IgM. Therefore, CAD can promote hemolytic destruction of erythrocytes through opsonization by macrophages in the spleen or liver. In contrast, autoantibodies that bind to erythrocytes at warmer temperatures are typically IgG. These temperature-dependent differences in antibody binding between IgM and IgG appear to reflect the thermodynamic differences between polysaccharide and peptide binding, respectively, as recognition of peptide antigens can involve hydrophobic interactions, which are relatively strong non-covalent forces not found in interactions between polysaccharides. CAD is a relapsing disease in which the severity of the disease fluctuates depending on the concentration of antibodies and how well they can activate complement. CAD may occur as a result of an underlying condition, such as a result of an infection or undiagnosed lymphoproliferative disorder. CAD is often associated with infections by either the Epstein-Barr virus, the cause of infectious mononucleosis, or M. pneumoniae, the primary cause of atypical pneumonia.  For patients who suffer debilitating symptoms due to CAD, avoidance of cold temperatures is paramount, including drinking cold liquids or being in cold rooms. Severe anemia can be treated with transfusions and plasmapheresis to remove the IgM molecules serving as cold agglutinins. It bears mentioning that all intravenous solutions should be warmed to body temperature yet must not exceed 40 degrees centigrade before infusion. Patients with CAD should undergo evaluation for an underlying disorder; antimicrobial therapy, for example, is warranted if an infection is associated with the condition. CAD may also occur from other primary causes, such as non-Hodgkin lymphoma, and therefore, flow cytometry of leukocytes biopsied from the bone marrow is useful in this regard. Rituximab is very useful for the treatment of debilitating CAD that is not associated with a malignancy, and the ability to treat CAD with inhibitors of complement is an area of active investigation. agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Immunoglobulin M -- Clinical Significance -- Selective IgM Deficiency
agent1_snippets.content: Selective IgM deficiency (SIGMD) is a rare disorder with fewer than 300 cases reported. SIGMD is associated with an isolated deficiency in IgM in the presence of normal levels of other immunoglobulins such as IgG and IgA and normal levels of T cells and other leukocytes. [12] Individuals with SIGMD may be asymptomatic, or they may suffer from recurring infections from encapsulated bacteria (eg, S. pneumoniae and H. influenzae ) in addition to viral infections. Additionally, SIGMD can be associated with malignancy, autoimmunity, or allergy. SIGMD may be a secondary effect of another disease, such as malignancy or bacteremia. Yet, primary causes of SIGMD have also been described, as some are associated with deletions on chromosome 22, for example. [12] The diagnosis of SIGMD is one of exclusion. Other diseases that result in low levels of multiple isotypes must be excluded, such as common variable immunodeficiency or X-linked agammaglobulinemia, which likely result in reduced levels of several antibody isotypes. Conversely, Wiskott-Aldrich syndrome is often associated with low IgM yet elevated levels of IgG and IgA. Cold Agglutinin Disease

agent1_snippets.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Pathophysiology -- Cold Agglutinin Disease
agent1_snippets.content: Cold agglutinin disease is a rare autoimmune disorder characterized by autoantibodies, primarily of the IgM class, which exhibit reactivity against RBCs at temperatures below normal body temperature. Occasionally, IgG and IgA cold agglutinins have been reported, although IgA cold agglutinin does not cause cold agglutinin disease. The pathophysiology involves a complex interplay of immune mechanisms that culminate in the destruction of RBCs and subsequent anemia. [11] [18] [19] The underlying trigger for cold agglutinin disease is the production of autoantibodies by the immune system. [20] These autoantibodies typically target antigens, such as the I/i, GLOB, or P antigens, on the surface of RBCs.

agent1_snippets.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: In symptomatic patients with cold agglutinin disease, whether or not a low-grade bone marrow lymphoproliferative disorder is detected, first-line treatment typically involves immunosuppressive therapy with rituximab monotherapy or a rituximab-bendamustine combination. [57] Paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria is managed similarly to cold AIHA to avoid cold exposure; however, the efficacy of glucocorticoids has not been established. [10] The cornerstone of treatment in patients with DIIHA involves discontinuation of the offending medication. Symptomatic cases may benefit from glucocorticoid therapy. [58] [59] Severe and relapsing cases may require further therapies such as IVIG therapeutic plasma exchange and/or rituximab.

agent1_snippets.title: Biochemistry, Immunoglobulin M -- Clinical Significance -- Selective IgM Deficiency
agent1_snippets.content: Cold agglutinin disease (CAD) is a form of autoimmune hemolytic anemia (AHA) mediated predominantly by IgM antibodies. As the name of the disease implies, IgM recognizing these erythrocyte antigens can agglutinate erythrocytes at colder temperatures. IgG or IgM antibodies that bind to erythrocytes can lead to red blood cell destruction by either promoting opsonization and clearance of erythrocytes by phagocytes in the spleen or liver or by activating complement leading to erythrocyte lysis as a result of MAC formation. Autoantibodies can cause AHA, can be induced by an infection, or can be drug-induced, in which case antibodies recognize complexes of drugs bound to erythrocytes. CAD is a rare disease affecting approximately 16 per million and is predominantly associated with IgM autoantibodies directed against the blood group antigens large I and small i. [13] [14] These ubiquitously expressed antigens are polysaccharides, and due to their relatively weak non-covalent interactions with IgM, IgM binding to the antigens is significantly strengthed at lower temperatures when erythrocytes circulate in the periphery.

agent1_snippets.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: All IgA deficient patines, even if asymptomatic, should receive polyvalent pneumococcal and influenza vaccines; patients must avoid attenuated or live vaccines. Specifically, live vaccines such as oral polio, Bacille-Calmette-Guerin, and yellow fever, should be considered contraindicated due to the threat of subsequent severe infection.

agent1_snippets.title: Selective IgA Deficiency -- History and Physical
agent1_snippets.content: Some autoimmune diseases can have manifestations that can cloud or cover IgA deficiency necessitating an additional workup. Certain conditions like ataxia-telangiectasia can be problematic based on their multiple immunoglobulin deficiencies (IgA, IgG, and IgE are all decreased, unlike in IgA deficiency, where the IgG is normal). Patients with Hyper-IgM syndrome will present with decreased IgA as part of a hypergammaglobulinemia where only the IgM is elevated. These patients present with severe infections and hyperviscosity (due to the increased IgM) and are inherited in either an X-linked or autosomal recessive format. [23] Selective IgA deficiency should be a possibility in every workup of an autoimmune disease.

agent1_snippets.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Diagnostic Tests -- Direct Antiglobulin Test or Direct Coombs Test
agent1_snippets.content: In patients with hemolytic anemia lacking recent transfusions or cold agglutinin or Donath-Landsteiner antibodies, a positive DAT for IgG and/or C3d is typically indicative of warm AIHA. Cold agglutinin disease is mainly characterized by DAT positivity for C3d (usually negative or weakly positive for IgG), indicating the involvement of IgM antibodies. For clinical significance, cold agglutinin titers are typically more than 512; however, levels above 64 are considered positive. Mixed AIHA manifests as DAT positivity for both IgG and high-titer cold agglutinins. Additionally, atypical forms of AIHA may occur, presenting as DAT-negative cases driven by IgA or warm IgM antibodies. [15]

agent1_snippets.content: However, there are a few drawbacks to the use of IVIG. Firstly, even though IVIG protects against most of the common pathogens, a few uncommon ones to which the donor pool has not been exposed are not protected against. Secondly, out of all types of immunoglobulins, only IgG is replaced while IgA and IgM are not and have their own unique functions. Finally, passive immunity through IVIG does not replace the rise in immunoglobulins seen in a healthy individual after exposure to foreign antigens. [10]

agent1_snippets.content: Even though immunoglobulin replacement is a safe and effective treatment strategy for these patients, Hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) is an alternative. The tedious procedure of HSCT and the difficult procurement of a suitable donor are additional factors that make it a less popular treatment option. agent1_snippets.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Introduction
agent1_snippets.content: Immune hemolytic anemias are categorized into autoimmune, alloimmune, and drug-induced mechanisms, each with unique etiologies and pathophysiological processes. Autoimmune hemolytic anemias (AIHAs) include warm AIHA, cold agglutinin disease, and mixed-type AIHA. The most common form of AIHA is warm AIHA, which is mediated by immunoglobulin G (IgG) antibodies and is often associated with hematologic, autoimmune, and infectious conditions. Cold agglutinin disease involves IgM antibodies that react at cold temperatures, and paroxysmal cold hemoglobinuria features an IgG autoantibody causing hemolysis upon rewarming. Alloimmune hemolytic anemia occurs when alloantibodies attack foreign RBC antigens, typically following blood transfusions or during hemolytic disease of the newborn. Drug-induced immune hemolytic anemia (DIIHA) occurs when drug-induced antibodies bind to and destroy RBCs.

agent1_snippets.title: Agammaglobulinemia -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: It protects against most of the pathogens, but protection against uncommon pathogens is limited if the donor pool has not been exposed to them. During treatment, only IgG is replaced while the rest of the immunoglobulins are not; these include IgA, and IgM, which have their unique functions, particularly protecting mucosal surfaces. Replacement IVIG therapy is very costly and not sustainable – especially in areas with limited resources.

agent1_snippets.title: Common Variable Hypogammaglobulinemia (Archived) -- Evaluation
agent1_snippets.content: The European society for immunodeficiencies (ESID) clinical criteria to diagnose CVID are listed below: Diagnosis can only be made after the fourth year of life. We have excluded the secondary cause of hypogammaglobulinemia as mentioned above and no evidence of profound T-cell deficiency. And at least one of the following: Increased susceptibility to infection Autoimmune manifestations Granulomatous disease Unexplained polyclonal lymphoproliferation The affected family members with antibody deficiency And marked decrease of IgG and marked decrease of IgA with or without low IgM levels (<2 SD of the normal levels for their age) with at least 2 measurements And at least one of the following: poor antibody response to vaccines, i.e., absence of protective levels despite vaccination where defined low switched memory B cells. [30]

agent1_snippets.title: Immunoglobulin -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Immunoglobulins or antibodies are essential in protecting against bacteria, viruses, and fungi. When there is a deficiency of these glycoproteins, recurrent infectious diseases occur, as seen in the following antibody deficiency disorders [16] : X-linked agammaglobulinemia Transient hypogammaglobulinemia of infancy IgA deficiency IgG subclass deficiency Immunodeficiency with increased IgM Common variable immunodeficiency

agent1_snippets.title: Laboratory Evaluation of Immune Hemolytic Anemias -- Clinical Significance
agent1_snippets.content: Diagnosing the specific type of hemolytic anemia is crucial for tailoring effective management strategies and optimizing patient outcomes. Distinguishing between different etiologies, such as AIHA, alloimmune hemolytic anemia, and other forms of hemolysis, guides treatment decisions and interventions. Identifying whether the condition involves warm or cold antibody-mediated mechanisms in AIHA influences treatment selection. Warm AIHA, characterized by IgG-mediated hemolysis, typically responds well to initial treatment with glucocorticoids and rituximab, particularly in symptomatic patients. [3] Conversely, cold AIHA, involving IgM antibodies and complement activation, may necessitate different therapeutic approaches, such as avoidance of cold exposure and, in severe cases, plasmapheresis or immunosuppressive therapy.

agent2_snippets.content: The category of autoimmune disorders (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية) encompasses several distinct conditions, including التهاب المفاصل (arthritis), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia), and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation). Each of these diseases is characterized by an inappropriate immune response that targets the body’s own tissues, leading to joint inflammation, destruction of red blood cells, or inflammation of the coronary arteries. Recognizing that these diverse clinical entities share a common autoimmune basis aids clinicians in diagnosing, monitoring, and applying immunomodulatory therapies across this spectrum of immune‑mediated illnesses.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1385
agent2_snippets.content: The SMQ category of immune disorders/autoimmune comprises a diverse group of hematologic and rheumatologic conditions. Within this category, aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is recognized as an autoimmune-mediated failure of bone‑marrow production. Non‑regenerative anemia (فقر الدم اللا تنسجي) is also classified as an autoimmune disorder in which the marrow does not adequately replace red blood cells. Acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب) and its cold‑agglutinin subtype (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد) are both autoimmune hemolytic processes in which antibodies target red blood cells, leading to their premature destruction. In addition, inflammatory arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) is included as an autoimmune joint disease characterized by chronic inflammation and tissue damage. Together, these entities illustrate how autoimmune mechanisms can affect both blood formation and joint health, underscoring the importance of recognizing their shared immunologic basis for accurate diagnosis and coordinated patient management.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2241
agent2_snippets.content: Autoimmune disorders classified under اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ) encompass a diverse range of conditions that share an underlying immune‑mediated pathophysiology. فقر الدم الوبيل (warm autoimmune hemolytic anemia) is included in this group, reflecting the immune system’s attack on red blood cells leading to hemolysis. فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold agglutinin disease) is also a member, representing a similar hemolytic process that is triggered by cold temperatures. التهاب المفاصل (arthritis) falls within the same SMQ category, illustrating how autoimmune mechanisms can target synovial joints and cause chronic inflammation and pain. Finally, التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation) is part of اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ), highlighting that autoimmune activity can affect vascular structures and contribute to coronary artery disease. Recognizing these entities—warm and cold autoimmune hemolytic anemias, arthritis, and coronary artery inflammation—as members of a common autoimmune disorder group underscores their shared immunological basis and supports integrated diagnostic and therapeutic approaches in patient care.

This categorization reflects that each of these diseases involves dysregulated immune activity that targets the body’s own tissues—whether the bone marrow in aplastic anemia, synovial joints in arthritis, red blood cells in the two forms of hemolytic anemia, or the coronary arterial wall in coronary artery inflammation. Recognizing these conditions as part of a common autoimmune spectrum highlights shared pathogenic pathways and can guide clinicians toward integrated diagnostic approaches and therapeutic strategies that address underlying immune dysfunction.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1482
agent2_snippets.content: The classification “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” encompasses several distinct conditions that share an autoimmune basis. Within this group, التهاب المفاصل is recognized as an autoimmune form of arthritis, فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد represents cold‑agglutinin autoimmune hemolytic anemia, and التهاب الشريان التاجي denotes inflammation of the coronary arteries driven by autoimmune mechanisms. Although فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية is listed among these entities, it is in fact a hemolytic anemia that does not arise from autoimmunity and therefore should be distinguished from the true autoimmune members of the “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” category. Recognizing which disorders are truly autoimmune is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic targeting, and optimal patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1239
agent2_snippets.content: The collection “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ)” comprises a range of hematologic and inflammatory conditions that share an autoimmune basis. Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل) is considered an autoimmune disorder, as are autoimmune hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية) and cold‑type hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد). Arthritis (التهاب المفاصل) and coronary artery inflammation (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are also members of this autoimmune group, illustrating the systemic nature of immune‑mediated disease. Although non‑immune hemolytic anemia (فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية) is listed among these entities, it does not result from autoantibody‑mediated red‑cell destruction; its inclusion reflects clinical overlap and underscores the importance of distinguishing immune from non‑immune mechanisms when evaluating hemolysis. Recognizing that these diverse conditions are linked by an underlying autoimmune process helps clinicians diagnose, monitor, and manage patients with immune‑driven pathology more effectively.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2177
agent2_snippets.content: Aplastic anemia (فقر الدم الوبيل), arthritis (التهاب المفاصل), acquired hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب), autoimmune‑mediated hemolytic anemia (فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية), and coronary arteritis (التهاب الشريان التاجي) are all classified as members of the autoimmune disorder group (اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية SMQ). These conditions share a common pathophysiology in which the immune system mistakenly attacks self‑tissues, leading to hematologic abnormalities in the various anemias and inflammatory damage in joints and coronary arteries. Recognizing this shared autoimmune basis guides clinicians toward similar diagnostic approaches—such as autoantibody testing—and therapeutic strategies, including immunosuppressive or immunomodulatory treatments, while emphasizing the need to monitor for systemic involvement across both blood‑forming and vascular systems.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 931
agent2_snippets.content: Anemia in chronic kidney disease is classified as an “Other deficiency anemia,” a broad category that also includes فقر الدم الوبيل (aplastic anemia), فقر الدم حديدي الأرومات (iron‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية (autoimmune hemolytic anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑type hemolytic anemia), and فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (non‑immune hemolytic anemia). All of these anemia subtypes, together with التهاب المفاصل (arthritis) and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation), are listed as members of اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ), indicating their relevance to autoimmune or immune‑mediated disease processes. Although فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية is grouped within this SMQ category, its pathogenesis is non‑immune, representing an exception within the otherwise immune‑related group. Prednisone may be used to treat فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية, highlighting a therapeutic link between immune modulation and specific anemia types. Recognizing these interconnections aids clinicians in diagnosing, categorizing, and managing the related hematologic and inflammatory conditions within an immunologic framework.

This therapeutic option is important for managing patients whose red blood cell destruction is driven by an autoimmune process.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 2 -- Community 1115
agent2_snippets.content: Other deficiency anemias is a broad classification that includes several distinct anemia entities. It is classified as فقر الدم الناتج عن نقص الفولات (folate‑deficiency anemia), فقر الدم الوبيل (pernicious anemia), فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية (autoimmune hemolytic anemia), and فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية (non‑immune hemolytic anemia). Within this group, فقر الدم اللا تنسجي (aplastic anemia) and فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب (acquired hemolytic anemia) are identified as child subcategories, and it has a specific relationship with فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد (cold‑agglutinin hemolytic anemia). All of these anemia types—فقر الدم الوبيل, فقر الدم اللا تنسجي, فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب, فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية, فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد, and فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية—are members of the اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ) group, which also includes التهاب المفاصل (arthritis). This organization highlights the close association of many deficiency‑related anemias with autoimmune mechanisms, underscoring their relevance for diagnostic categorization and integrated patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 4 -- Community 2694
agent2_snippets.content: Dextran 70, a high‑molecular‑weight plasma volume expander, is contraindicated in patients with الاعتلال الخثري (thrombotic disease). Because Dextran 70 can increase blood viscosity and promote coagulation, its use in individuals who already have a propensity for clot formation may exacerbate the underlying thrombotic condition and increase the risk of serious vascular complications. Clinicians should therefore avoid administering Dextran 70 to patients diagnosed with الاعتلال الخثري and consider alternative volume‑expanding therapies that do not carry this pro‑thrombotic risk.
